speaker: interviewer
text: This is the May 15th, 1995 interview of Jenny Singer. This is tape number 3. You were discussing your life just after the war. But let me go back, if I might, for a second and ask you if you ever were able to obtain any information regarding what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I was told that they just saw my mother in [? Sdolbonov. ?] An author of this book-- matter of fact, I have the book too-- They mentioned that the good German, you know, he somehow brought Jews. He-- he said that he needs-- I don't remember how many-- hundreds of them. I think he saved about 800 Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he brought them all to a ghetto in [? Sdolbonov, ?] which was very close to Rovno. And they said that they saw my mother, and my mother kept asking, maybe you know something about my daughters? But nobody knew anything about us. But later on, some people that survived told me that my mother kept asking all the time when they saw her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she perished. Apparently she perished in the ghetto, because we didn't hear anything from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: We left off, you were-- I believe you were explaining that you had taken some courses in Germany, which may have helped you later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I went to [NON-ENGLISH] University in Munich, and I studied. Every day I studied, and I have a day in order to [INAUDIBLE]-- even when I was married. But my husband [? doesn't ?] [? fare ?] [? well, ?] and so it was very difficult, you know? And he hardly made any money. But I stayed with some friends in Munich, and I had the support to-- to help myself along. So I cleaned the university rooms after-- after they [? studied. ?] And then when I conceived with my son. And in the last month, I-- I stopped studying, and my son was born. I didn't study anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned that your daughter was born. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born [INAUDIBLE] in the United States, 1951. We came to the United States, December 2, 19-- 1940-- 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Explain that to me, if you would. You-- you-- how-- how it is that you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father and-- and my husband in Germany worked in [? Schleissheim ?] for the [? train ?] distribution. You know, there were American-- American companies and South African Jews that contributed clothing for the Jews in the displaced camp. And my husband started to work. And he worked himself up, and he became a manager of the shoe department. And before we came to the United States, one of the directors, an African Jew, gave my husband a-- a very nice letter of recommendation. He said that his integrity and his honesty and that he was a very good worker, put in his many hours and more. And that's the way we came to the United States. And as I said, when we came to the United States, the Joint Distribution gave us an apartment. And they said my husband should start to look for work. But he said, he wants to work right away. He doesn't want to accept any-- any relief, any charity from anybody. And they gave him a job. And in [? yeshiva, ?] he peeled potatoes, and he cleaned. He cleaned everything. And later, he met a man, [NON-ENGLISH], who had a factory-- a ladies-- children's wear, Broadway. And he took him in, and he was working. And after the working hours, he taught him how to become a cutter. And we moved to an unfurnished apartment to-- to a regular apartment in the Bronx. First we lived in 1259 East 13th Street in Brooklyn. And after a year and a half, one of our friends moved out. They bought themselves a house-- friends always from back home. And we moved into their apartment. It was 134 East Mosholu Parkway. And my husband was working. And I had the children, and I couldn't work. But still, in order to help out, you know, I was babysitting. Some people that did go to work brought in their children, and I kept them together. And then we saved. We saved, and then-- and then when children grew up, I went to go too. I went to school, and I picked up bookkeeping, stenography, and typing. And I got myself a bookkeeping job. And we saved up money, and we bought ourselves a house. My children went to school. Both of them graduated. And my son is a psychologist. My daughter is a teacher, and she has a very good job. She's teaching in Queens. She lives in Long Island. She has two children. One is 17 years old. And one is-- the daughter is 13 and 1/2. My son, he said, to all the atrocities, whatever. In the beginning, I was numb. I couldn't tell him anything. But later on, he start asking me, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived in the Bronx. We lived in a high stoop. There was a-- there was a basement apartment and a high stoop. And I remember the holidays came. It  was Saturday and Sunday. And it was a mixed-- a mixed population, Jewish and Italian. And grandparents used to come and uncles and aunts. And my children used to ask me, why don't we-- where are our grandparents? Where are our aunts and uncles? And little by little, you know, I tried to reveal to them. When my son was three month-- three years old, he asked me once what happened. Where are my parents? And I told him that my parents were killed by bad people. And he said, and where was the policeman? At three years, he asked, where are the policemen?

speaker: interviewee
text: But as he grew up, you know, it was difficult for me to talk. But I did prepare books. And I gave my son to read and and And sure enough, when the '67-- when the '73 war broke out in Israel, my son enlisted. I was-- and I am-- proud and fond of him. But as a parent, you know, I said, gosh, you are the only family. [INAUDIBLE] you and your sister. And I feel something will happen to you, [INAUDIBLE] happen. And he said, Ma, you are proud when you pass by the United Nations and you see among so many flags an Israeli flag. This is where-- it's where it is hanging through. And the Israeli mothers also cry over their children. I have to go. It's my duty. And he went. He went. He was a whole year. We used to call. My boss let me call. I worked in one office for 21 years. And my boss said, any time you feel if you can't get connected to your son, just call him. And I called him. And he said that as an American citizen, he couldn't fight. But he delivered arms to the front And he told me he was delivering rice to the front. And he lives now in Israel. Has the children, married an American girl-- struggles. But he said, we have to have a country. And if we would have it, Israel would became-- would be independent years earlier. 6 million Jews wouldn't perish. Because the whole free world closed all the gates to all the doors for us, and we didn't have where to go. It was very difficult-- very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you, to what do you attribute the fact that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would say pure luck. Just pure luck, because my sister was smarter than I was. And she was a beautiful--looking girl, you know? I am not a religious person, but I believe in Providence because I faced death so many times-- so many times. And somehow, I always escaped. And it was very, very difficult. It is difficult for me now to talk. But it is my duty, as my mother wanted us to survive. My sister didn't-- so sorry that she did not survive. Because I am a survivor, and I have to do something. Another Hitler shouldn't get up. Even-- you know, I-- there are many deniers that deny the Holocaust, no matter what they see. I myself teach Holocaust survivor studies. And most students are very sympathetic. They kiss me. They hug me. They-- they thank me for enlightening them. And they feel sorry for me because many times, I lose my composure and start to cry for a brief time. And I beg them  they should be goodwill ambassadors. They should fight not only for-- for-- for anti-Semitism, but for prejudice. Because prejudice and hate brings, leads to, things like this. And you know, I-- I tell them that how Hitler, when he wrote how he attributed the German race as a superior race. And he wanted to produce only a clean race-- blonde hair and blue eyes. And once when I had the class-- and most, you know, it was like a melting pot. There were African-Americans. There were Indians. There were Oriental people. There were Chinese people. And myself and two Jewish girls were light hair. And I said, you know, if Hitler would really win the War, you wouldn't have-- the only-- the only survivors would be two Jewish girls and myself. And I said, take a look what Hitler did. We-- we survived. So sorry that very few of us survived, but we survived. We have another country. We are loyal and very good citizens of the United States because this country was good for us, but we also understand-- and have to understand-- the great importance of the state of Israel. Because the United States is a melting pot from all nationalities. And all the nationalities have a country, a mother country. And we are very proud of our country. And we have to make sure it should exist forever and ever and be strong and independent. And we have to try to make sure there will be future generations of Jewish people who continue to survive, make contributions to other-- to a better world that another Holocaust should never, never take place. I have to try.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the reason in my culture, my daughter always asked me, Ma, why do we have to read all the books? Why do you have to go and see journalists? I said, I am the lucky one. I am the one that survived the War. I am the lucky one that-- that gave birth to two children. And now, you have children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember once going to work, and I read the book on the subway-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which stands for it. And-- and somehow, I lost my composure, and I started to cry on the subway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw that somebody put arms and looked at me. And I-- I turned my head, and I saw my boss. And he said, why do you have to torture yourself? Why do you have to read that book? I said, you know not why not? To prove it to myself how lucky I am. Yes, that I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'll keep going on teaching Holocaust survivor studies, enlightening the world to tell them that the Holocaust did take place and that Hitler did not accomplish anything. That his race is all mixed up, that hundreds of thousands of Germans were killed, hundreds of thousands of-- I was in Germany after the war years-- not that I wanted but I had to stay until I had a chance to come to the United States. Hundreds of thousands were invalids. And they said on every corner, they said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And they were so polite.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at once I asked one, where was your god during the war? And some of them said, well, Hitler did many good things. I said, like what? They brought the destruction. I say-- it was in Munich. And I said, if you would take a look-- it was before the United States' Marshall Plan built up Germany because they wanted to make a democracy out of this country, yes? I said, take a look. You sit among ruins-- your ruins. Your people were killed. What did he accomplish? Maybe this will teach us a lesson forever and ever. I said, what were the good things? They said, well, the good things when we were defeated during World War I, Hitler gave us jobs. I said, eventually, with a little more patience, you would have jobs and would-- and you wouldn't have such a destruction that you have now. But our calamity, the calamity of the Jews-- at least the Germans got killed or wounded because they were fighting. But whom did we fight? We didn't fight anybody. Imagine 6 million Jews and 1 and 1/2 million people-- children that were cut down so young.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] And when I talk, I talk for their sake. It should never be forgotten. And I hope that the people that will listen-- that-- that will ever listen to the tape will see that there has to be-- have to build a just world. And you should treat everybody as human beings, because we all have the right to live on the planet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know it'll cost me a lot of health. I won't sleep nights. But-- but I felt I did something-- something that my mother wanted me to do. And if my sister didn't live through to do it, I had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that that has joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Phillip Singer, also a Holocaust survivor. We met in 1945 in Rovno, in my hometown. He couldn't take his city because he couldn't find anybody alive, and I couldn't wait to leave my city because every corner and every street kept-- kept bringing such sad memories. We got married, knowing each other very shortly. And as citizens of Poland, we had the right to leave. That time it was Russia, [INAUDIBLE], after 1939-- after 1941-- when the Russians took over again. And we married, and we came to the United States. We have two children-- as I said before-- a son and a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Rifka [NON-ENGLISH], when she was a young lady. I don't remember exactly when it was taken, because I obtained the picture from a second cousin from Israel. But there is an inscription on the other side. It was taken in Rovno, city where I was born and my mother. There's something [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, second to the left. Her name was [? Beverly ?] [? Dehover. ?] We were hiding together at the attic, and as I said before in the statement. And the Germans came and chased us out. I ran first, and apparently, they kept her. And I'm still looking for her. I doubt if I'll ever find her. But she was a beautiful, beautiful girl. The picture was taken from her classroom. The man in the middle was her friend. He is a survivor. He lives in Haifa, Israel, and he gave me the pictures. It was taken in-- in high school, in [NON-ENGLISH] High School in Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- this picture showed the-- the classroom, [? her ?] gymnasium where  my sister went to Gymnasium [NON-ENGLISH]. She is from the left-- the third from the left. And I obtained the picture through her friend. It was [? shown a ?] [? picture. ?] He is here some place also. [? Well, ?] he is a survivor. He lives in Haifa, and he gave me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandson's, David [? Humel's, ?] bar mitzvah, which took place four years ago. You can see David. You can see my daughter. Next to him is my daughter, Karen. The little one is my granddaughter, Barbara. That's myself and my husband, Phillip Singer. This is my son, Ronald Singer, who lives in Israel. And those are his two children. The little one that he embraces is Daniel. He's four years old. And the bigger one at the side is [NON-ENGLISH]. And he is five years old. To the other side is my grandson, David. He is now 17 years old. And my granddaughter, Barbara, who is 13 and 1/2 years old. Those are my daughter's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they don't have their picture there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jenny?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture-- this is-- this is a certificate from Coral Gables High School where I teach Holocaust survivor studies. I teach also in many different schools. And the aim is to tell the children that the Holocaust really took place; and to tell them all the atrocities; and to tell them to-- to fight prejudice, to fight indifference, to fight hatred-- to make sure that they build a world, a just world. And everybody should have the right to live peaceful. And everybody should [INAUDIBLE]. If not to love, at least there should be harmony among the nationalities. Because as I teach and I-- there are different school-- children from all different backgrounds, from all different nationalities. And somehow we tell them, take a look. Here is a class of so many hundreds of-- a-- a school of so many hundreds of children, and there is so much harmony. If you want, you can achieve. And I beg them, and I appeal to them. They should be our goodwill ambassadors. Because the children of today are the future leaders of tomorrow, and they should strive to create a just world-- a world of peace and harmony.
